CFDocument.creator: Georgia Department of Education
CFDocument.title: Physical Education
CFDocument.notes: The Physical Education framework includes all Physical Education courses with State Board of Education adopted standards as well as additional Physical Education Courses that are approved by the State Board of Education but do not have state-adopted content standards. Local educational agencies (LEAs) are responsible for developing the curriculum and instructional programming for these courses, ensuring alignment with the official course description.

CFItems.fullStatement: Physical Education/Grade K
Motor Skills and Movement Patterns
The physically educated student demonstrates competency in a variety of motor skills and movement patterns.
Locomotor
Performs basic locomotor skills (walk, run, hop, jump, skip, slide, and gallop) while maintaining balance.
Performs locomotor skills in response to teacher-led, creative dance.
Performs jumping/landing with balance.
Non-Locomotor
Performs basic non-locomotor skills (bend, straighten, twist, stretch, swing, sway, and turn) while maintaining balance.
Maintains balance on different bases of support (wide, narrow, curled, and twisted body shapes).
Manipulative Skills
Throws underhand with opposite foot forward.
Catches a self-tossed object (ball, scarf, and bean bag).
Drops a ball and catches it after a single bounce.
Demonstrates basic dribbling skills with hands attempting multiple contacts.
Demonstrates basic dribbling skills with feet attempting multiple contacts.
Kicks a stationary ball from a stationary position (keeping eyes on the ball, stepping with the opposite foot next to ball, contacting the ball with dominant foot, and following through).
Volleys a lightweight object (beach ball or balloon) upward using a variety of body parts.
Strikes a lightweight object (beach ball or balloon) using a short-handled implement.
Executes a single jump using a self-turned rope.
Movement Concepts and Principles
The physically educated student applies the knowledge of concepts, principles, strategies, and tactics related to movement and performance.
Demonstrates an understanding of general and personal space by traveling without bumping into another student.
Performs a rhythmic activity in a personal space.
Travels in three different pathways (straight, curved, and zig zag)
Travels in general space with different speeds.
Travels in general space on different levels.
Fitness

Identifies active play opportunities outside the physical education setting.
Actively participates in physical education class.

Personal and Social Behavior, Rules, Safety, and Etiquette
The physically educated student exhibits responsible personal and social behavior that respects self and others in physical activity settings.
Follows directions individually and in a group setting (follows rules and takes turns).
Acknowledges responsibility for behavior when prompted.
Shares equipment and space with others.
Recognizes the established classroom procedures.
Follows teacher directions for safe participation and proper use of equipment with minimal reminders.
The physically educated student recognizes the value of physical activity for health, enjoyment, challenge, self-expression, and/or social interaction.
Recognizes that physical activity is important for good health.

Identifies physical activities that are enjoyable.
Recognizes the enjoyment of playing with others.
Accepts and respects differences and similarities of physical abilities in self and others.
High School Physical Education 9-12
CFItems.notes:
On May 3, 2018, the State Board of Education approved the K-12 Georgia Standards of Excellence (GSE) for Physical Education.

The use of the revised GSE for Physical Education was voluntary for the 2018-2019 school year. Full implementation began in the 2019-2020 school year.

CFItems.fullStatement: General Physical Education II
CFItems.notes:
General Physical Education II 36.01200
Course Description: Enhances level-one skills in any different combination or variety of team sports, lifetime sports, track and field events, aquatics/water sports, outdoor education experiences, rhythmic/dance, recreational games, gymnastics, and self-defense; and further promotes methods to attain a healthy and active lifestyle.

***The physically educated student** has developmentally appropriate grade-level comprehension and application of the concepts and principles of fitness motor skills, movement patterns, tactics, and strategies which contribute to a physically literate and active lifestyle.*

Motor Skills and Movement Patterns
The physically educated student demonstrates competency in a variety of motor skills and movement patterns.
Demonstrates competence while performing skills in a variety of settings or activities, including sport, rhythms, and other lifetime and recreational activities.
Demonstrates the proper warm-up and cool-down protocol associated with different physical activities.
Movement Concepts and Principles
The physically educated student applies the knowledge of concepts, principles, strategies, and tactics related to movement and performance.
Integrates a variety of strategies, tactics, concepts, and skills during sports and activities.
Creates a sequence of movements (dance, gymnastics, sports, etc.) that transition and flow smoothly from one to the next.
Evaluates skills needed for sports, outdoor, rhythm, and lifetime leisure activities.
Fitness
The physically educated student demonstrates knowledge and skills to help achieve and maintain a health-enhancing level of physical activity and fitness.
Maintains or improves fitness level by using the results of the Georgia Fitness Assessment to guide a personal program of physical activity.
Uses technologies to assess, enhance, and maintain health-related and skill-related fitness.
Analyzes the relationship between physical activity and longevity.
Evaluates the relationship between exercise (fitness) and nutrition.
Develops fitness goals that are age, gender, and skill appropriate.

Displays the ability to design rules, procedures, and routines appropriate for the group.
Demonstrates the ability to apply the rules and etiquette of various physical activities regardless of differences.
Applies safe practices in the physical education setting.
Describes safety protocol to avoid dehydration, overexertion, and hypo/hyperthermia during physical activity.

Physical Eduation Courses with Adopted Standards
Lifeguarding and Aquatic Safety
Motor Skills and Movement Patterns
The physically educated student demonstrates competency in a variety of motor skills and movement patterns.
Demonstrates proficiency in combinations of motor skills related to aquatics.
Demonstrates proficiency and performs aquatic safety skills at a level of competence that contributes to water safety and lifeguarding.

Demonstrates proficiency in CPR, First Aid, and AED skills, including ventilations, one-rescuer and two-rescuer CPR, conscious and unconscious choking responses, AED operations, understanding of sudden illnesses, head, neck, and spinal injuries, and control of external bleeding.

Demonstrates all required lifeguarding rescue skills in accordance with national, evidence-based lifeguarding and aquatic safety guidelines from one of the following approved programs or providers:
-American Red Cross
-American Lifeguard Association
-StarGuard Elite
-Stop Drowning Now -Other evidence-based providers (e.g., YMCA)
Movement Concept of Principles
The physically educated student applies the knowledge of concepts, principles, strategies, and tactics related to aquatic safety and lifeguarding.
Applies concepts and terminology associated with lifeguarding and aquatic safety.
Evaluates the skills and strategies needed for various water safety and lifeguarding rescue scenarios.

Assesses the risks and safety considerations associated with various aquatic activities in varying environmental conditions, including heat, humidity, and cold (e.g., sun exposure, dehydration, heat cramps, heat exhaustion, heat stroke, and hypothermia).
Assesses the risks and safety considerations associated with aquatic conditions (e.g., water quality, current, tides, wildlife, water temperature, visibility, first aid accessibility).
Fitness
The physically educated student demonstrates knowledge and skills to help achieve and maintain a health-enhancing level of physical activity and fitness.
Demonstrates endurance, strength, and flexibility necessary for aquatic movement and safety tasks (e.g., physical competency swim).
Demonstrates the physical ability to respond effectively to emergencies and carry out essential life-saving actions. (e.g., treading water, brick retrieval, and rescue swimming)
Explores the physical benefits of maintaining an active lifestyle in the context of aquatic safety and lifeguarding.
Explores the physical benefits of maintaining their lifeguard certification through in-services (swim and skill practice).
Personal and Social Behavior, Rules, Safety, and Etiquette
The physically educated student exhibits responsible personal and social behavior that respects self and others in physical activity settings.

Understands and applies best practices to ensure safety while participating in and around aquatic activities. (e.g., using proper gear, staying alert, knowing rescue techniques, avoiding risky behaviors, adhering to safety guidelines).
Demonstrates proper etiquette and shows respect for others while participating in and around aquatic activities.
Applies safe practices in all aquatic activities.
Demonstrates effective verbal and non-verbal communication required in all aquatic scenarios, including simulated emergency responses.
The physically educated student recognizes the value of physical activity for health, enjoyment, challenge, self-expression, and/or social interaction.
Performs a basic water rescue, with or without equipment, including simulations of real-life scenarios (e.g., distressed swimmer, submerged victim, spinal injury).
Applies critical thinking and problem-solving skills in both independent and team-based aquatic scenarios.
